sections.heading: CONTEXT OF THE PROPOSAL
sections.title: Regulation Evaluation Timeline and Scope
sections.summary: Four years after the Regulation's entry into force, the Commission must evaluate the Regulation and submit a report to the European Parliament, Council, and European Economic and Social Committee. The evaluation assesses data accessibility categories, excluded data categories, contractual practices, financial data sharing schemes, and the impact of compensation on data users.

requirements.text: By [OP please insert the date = 4 years after the date of entry into application of this Regulation, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation and submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council as well as to the European Economic and Social Committee.

That evaluation shall assess, in particular: (a) other categories or sets of data to be made accessible; (b) the exclusion from the scope of certain categories of data and entities; (c) changes in contractual practices of data holders and data users and the operation of financial data sharing schemes; (d) the inclusion of other types of entities to those entities granted the right of access to data; (e) the impact of compensation on the ability of data users to participate in financial data sharing schemes and access data from data holders.

sections.source_quote: By [OP please insert the date = 4 years after the date of entry into application of this Regulation, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation and submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council as well as to the European Economic and Social Committee.

sections.heading: RESULTS
sections.title: Revocation of delegated powers
sections.summary: The European Parliament or Council may revoke the delegation of powers at any time, with revocation taking effect the day after publication in the Official Journal of the European Union. Revocation does not affect the validity of previously adopted delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for revocation of delegated powers by the European Parliament or Council, ensuring regulatory flexibility and oversight of delegated authority in financial data transfer rules.

It shall take effect the day following the publication of the decision in the Official Journal of the European Union or at a later date specified therein. It shall not affect the validity of any delegated acts already in force.

sections.source_quote: The delegation of powers referred to in Article 11, may be revoked at any time by the European Parliament or by the Council.

sections.heading: RESULTS (cont.)
sections.title: Financial data access economic impact assessment
sections.summary: This section provides a macroeconomic assessment of the potential economic benefits and costs of enhanced financial data access. It states that the proposal could generate annual benefits for the EU economy between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion, including EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion for the financial data economy. The proposal also involves one-off costs of EUR 2.2-2.4 billion and recurring annual costs of EUR 147-465 million. The assessment notes that while the proposal supports sustainable investment and inclusive finance, its economic impact is difficult to quantify precisely due to limited data availability and overlapping policy effects.
sections.dtia_summary: This section describes potential economic benefits and costs of financial data sharing but does not specify technical safeguards or transfer conditions relevant to DTIAs. It is not directly applicable to data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: The total annual benefits for the EU economy produced by enhanced access to and sharing of data in the EU financial sector ranges between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion
The overall estimated cost of the proposal could be up to a range of EUR 2.2 billion to EUR 2.4 billion in one-off costs and between EUR 147 million to EUR 465 million in recurring annual costs

sections.source_quote: According to this macroeconomic assessment, the total annual benefits for the EU economy produced by enhanced access to and sharing of data in the EU financial sector ranges between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion, including the direct impact on the EU financial data economy in the range of EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion per year.

sections.heading: BUDGETARY IMPLICATIONS
sections.title: Consultation of Member State Experts Before Delegated Act Adoption
sections.summary: Before adopting a delegated act, the European Commission must consult experts designated by each Member State. This consultation must follow the principles outlined in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making. The requirement ensures that Member State expertise is integrated into the legislative process prior to final adoption of delegated acts.

requirements.text: Before adopting a delegated act, the Commission shall consult experts signated by each Member State in accordance with the principles laid down in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making.

sections.source_quote: Before adopting a delegated act, the Commission shall consult experts signated by each Member State in accordance with the principles laid down in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS
sections.title: Cooperation with supervisory authorities for personal data processing
sections.summary: When obligations under this Regulation involve processing personal data, competent authorities must cooperate with supervisory authorities established under GDPR. This cooperation ensures that personal data processing activities comply with GDPR requirements across Member States.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that competent authorities work with GDPR supervisory authorities when handling personal data, which impacts DTIAs by requiring alignment with GDPR's data protection standards during cross-border financial data exchanges.

requirements.text: Where obligations under this Regulation concern the processing of personal data, competent authorities shall cooperate with the supervisory authorities established pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.source_quote: Where obligations under this Regulation concern the processing of personal data, competent authorities shall cooperate with the supervisory authorities established pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Customer data sharing in financial sector: permissions and safeguards
sections.summary: This section outlines the need for a regulatory framework enabling financial institutions to share customer data beyond payment accounts while ensuring customer control. It specifies that data sharing must be based on explicit customer permission, with data users acting on behalf of customers. The sharing must comply with GDPR principles including lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, and data minimisation. Customers retain the right to withdraw permission at any time, and data processing must respect confidentiality of business data and trade secrets.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires customer permission for data sharing beyond payment accounts, with specific safeguards to ensure data minimisation and GDPR compliance. Critical for DTIAs involving financial data transfers where customer consent is the sole lawful basis.

requirements.text: The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer.
The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.
Where the processing of personal data is involved, a data user should have a valid lawful basis for processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
The processing of personal data must respect the principles of personal data protection, including lawfulness, fairness and transparency, purpose limitation and data minimisation.
A customer has the right to withdraw the permission given to a data user.
When data processing is necessary for the performance of a contract, a customer should be able to withdraw permissions according to the contractual obligations to which the data subject is party.
When personal data processing is based on consent, a data subject has the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time, as provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.source_quote: The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer. The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Financial data sharing for customer access and consent management
sections.summary: This section establishes that financial institutions must provide defined categories of customer data upon request, while allowing data sharing based on customer consent. It specifies that financial institutions include those providing financial products or services, and mandates the creation of permission dashboards for customers to manage data access permissions. The dashboards must be accessible, impartial, and not influence customer decisions, with real-time updates on permission changes and a two-year retention period for withdrawn permissions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to implement permission dashboards for customers to manage data sharing permissions, ensuring transparency and control over cross-border data transfers. This impacts DTIA by mandating specific technical and governance requirements for data sharing mechanisms.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must provide defined categories of customer data at request
Data holders must provide customers with common and consistent financial data access permission dashboards
Permission dashboards must empower customers to manage permissions in an informed and impartial manner
Permission dashboards must not encourage or unduly influence customers to grant or withdraw permissions
Permission dashboards must display permissions given by customers, including when personal data are shared based on consent or are necessary for contract performance
Permission dashboards must warn customers of contractual consequences of permission withdrawal
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of any permission withdrawal
Permission dashboards must include a record of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions

sections.source_quote: Customers must have effective control over their data and confidence in managing permissions they have granted in accordance with this Regulation.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Financial data sharing schemes for cross-border data access
sections.summary: This section mandates the creation of financial data sharing schemes that establish standardized contractual frameworks, data interfaces, and governance rules for cross-border data sharing between financial institutions. These schemes must ensure proportionality, market competition, and consumer protection, with requirements for transparency, appeal procedures, and alignment with Union consumer and data protection rules. Financial information service providers must be authorized and supervised by competent authorities to access customer data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires the establishment of financial data sharing schemes with standardized interfaces and governance to facilitate cross-border data access while ensuring proportionality and consumer protection. This directly impacts DTIA by defining technical and regulatory requirements for international data sharing.

requirements.text: Data holders and data users must be part of financial data sharing schemes
Schemes must develop data and interface standards, joint contractual frameworks, and governance rules
Schemes must ensure inclusive governance with balanced representation of data holders, users, and customers
Schemes must have transparency requirements and a well-functioning appeal and review procedure
Schemes must comply with Union rules on consumer protection, data protection, privacy, and competition
Schemes must not impose restrictions that distort competition in a substantial market segment
Financial information service providers must be authorized and supervised by competent authorities
Financial information service providers must have strong cyber resilience standards

sections.source_quote: Data holders and data users should be required to be part of financial data sharing schemes.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Periodic penalties and expedited enforcement procedures for financial data breaches
sections.summary: Member States must establish specific rules and mechanisms for periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and their management bodies. Competent authorities can impose these penalties to compel compliance with orders, such as providing information or terminating breaches. Penalties must be calculated daily unless Member States specify otherwise. Member States may encourage expedited enforcement procedures but are not required to implement them, and must notify the Commission if they do so. Publication of penalties for legal entities is permitted to deter repeat infringements and inform cross-border risks, but must avoid disproportionate impacts on natural persons.
sections.dtia_summary: Periodic penalties and expedited enforcement procedures create conditions for financial data breach responses that impact cross-border data transfer assessments by requiring Member States to define penalty mechanisms and publication rules for financial entities.

requirements.text: Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and those members of their management body identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement.
Periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis unless otherwise provided for by Member States.
Member States may encourage expedited enforcement procedures but are not required to implement them.
Member States must notify the Commission if they empower competent authorities to use expedited enforcement procedures.
Publication of administrative penalties for legal entities is permitted to deter repeat infringements and inform cross-border risks.
Publication of penalties must consider the gravity of the infringement and the dissuasive effect.
Publication of penalties involving natural persons may impinge on their rights in a disproportionate manner.

sections.source_quote: Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions and regulatory coordination for financial data access
sections.summary: This Regulation does not affect existing financial services legislation on data access, including payment account data under Directive 2015/2366. It establishes a review clause for the Commission to assess impacts on access mechanisms for financial information service providers. The Regulation mandates amendments to EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA to ensure coverage under relevant financial regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions from existing financial data access rules and coordination with supervisory authorities create specific conditions for data transfer impact assessments in financial services.

requirements.text: This Regulation does not affect the provisions related to data access and data sharing in Union financial services legislation.
This Regulation establishes a review clause for the Commission to examine whether the introduction of rules impacts AISPs access to data.
EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA must be mandated to make use of their powers in relation to financial information service providers.

sections.source_quote: This Regulation does not affect the provisions related to data access and data sharing in Union financial services legislation.

sections.title: Data sharing requirements for data holders
sections.summary: When sharing customer data with data users, data holders must provide data in standardized formats, ensure secure communication, verify customer permission, provide permission dashboards for monitoring, and protect trade secrets and intellectual property rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies technical and procedural safeguards for data sharing, including data format standards, security requirements, and permission management, which are critical for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: make customer data available to the data user in a format based on generally recognised standards and at least in the same quality available to the data holder.

When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: communicate securely with the data user by ensuring an appropriate level of security for the processing and transmission of customer data.

When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: request data users to demonstrate that they have obtained the permission of the customer to access the customer data held by the data holder.
When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: provide the customer with a permission dashboard to monitor and manage permissions in accordance with Article 8.

When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: respect the confidentiality of trade secrets and intellectual property rights when customer data is accessed in accordance with Article 5(1).

sections.source_quote: When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: (a) make customer data available to the data user in a format based on generally recognised standards and at least in the same quality available to the data holder; (b) communicate securely with the data user by ensuring an appropriate level of security for the processing and transmission of customer data; (c) request data users to demonstrate that they have obtained the permission of the customer to access the customer data held by the data holder; (d) provide the customer with a permission dashboard to monitor and manage permissions in accordance with Article 8. (e) respect the confidentiality of trade secrets and intellectual property rights when customer data is accessed in accordance with Article 5(1).

sections.heading: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial
sections.title: Conditions for withdrawing financial information service provider authorisation
sections.summary: The competent authority may revoke authorization for financial information service providers if they fail to use the authorization within 12 months, expressly renounce it, cease business for over six months, obtained it through false statements, no longer meet authorization conditions, or fail to report major developments. The authority must provide reasons for withdrawal and notify affected parties, publishing the withdrawal in anonymized form.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which a regulator can withdraw authorization for financial data processors, impacting cross-border data transfers by requiring ongoing compliance and timely reporting to ensure continued data processing legitimacy.

requirements.text: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial information service provider only if the provider: (a) does not make use of the authorisation within 12 months, expressly renounces the authorisation or has ceased to engage in business for more than 6 months; (b) has obtained the authorisation through false statements or any other irregular means; (c) no longer meets the conditions for granting the authorisation or fails to inform the competent authority on major developments in this respect; (d) would constitute a risk to consumer protection and the security of data.
The competent authority shall give reasons for any withdrawal of an authorisation and shall inform those concerned accordingly.
The competent authority shall make public the withdrawal of an authorisation, in an anonymised version.

sections.source_quote: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial information service provider only if the provider: (a) does not make use of the authorisation within 12 months, expressly renounces the authorisation or has ceased to engage in business for more than 6 months; (b) has obtained the authorisation through false statements or any other irregular means; (c) no longer meets the conditions for granting the authorisation or fails to inform the competent authority on major developments in this respect; (d) would constitute a risk to consumer protection and the security of data.

sections.title: Consultation of Member State Experts Before Delegated Act Adoption
sections.summary: Before adopting a delegated act, the European Commission must consult experts designated by each Member State. This consultation must follow the principles outlined in the Interinstitutional Agreement of 13 April 2016 on Better Law-Making. The requirement ensures that Member State expertise is integrated into the legislative process prior to final adoption of delegated acts.

sections.title: Conditions for Delegated Act Entry into Force
sections.summary: A delegated act adopted under Article 11 enters into force only if no objections are raised by the European Parliament or Council within three months of notification. If objections are not raised, the act enters into force after the three-month period. The period may be extended by three months if either the European Parliament or Council initiates the extension. This requirement ensures legislative oversight and timely review of delegated acts.

requirements.text: A delegated act adopted pursuant to Article 11, shall enter into force only if no objection has been expressed either by the European Parliament or by the Council within a period of three months of notification of that act to the European Parliament and to the Council or if, before the expiry of that period, the European Parliament and the Council have both informed the Commission that they will not object.
That period shall be extended by three months on the initiative of the European Parliament or of the Council.

sections.title: Regulation Evaluation Timeline and Scope
sections.summary: Four years after the Regulation's entry into force, the Commission must evaluate the Regulation and submit a report to the European Parliament, Council, and European Economic and Social Committee. The evaluation assesses data accessibility categories, excluded data categories, contractual practices, financial data sharing schemes, and the impact of compensation on data users.

requirements.text: By [OP please insert the date = 4 years after the date of entry into application of this Regulation, the Commission shall carry out an evaluation of this Regulation and submit a report on its main findings to the European Parliament and to the Council as well as to the European Economic and Social Committee.

That evaluation shall assess, in particular: (a) other categories or sets of data to be made accessible; (b) the exclusion from the scope of certain categories of data and entities; (c) changes in contractual practices of data holders and data users and the operation of financial data sharing schemes; (d) the inclusion of other types of entities to those entities granted the right of access to data; (e) the impact of compensation on the ability of data users to participate in financial data sharing schemes and access data from data holders.
